summary: Significant high-risk regulatory changes include new, strict prohibitions in Japan on unapproved additives, various contaminants (e.g., toxic substances, excessive residues), non-conforming products, and those from diseased animals or with pathogen contamination/spoilage. Singapore has implemented stringent new rules for food additive approval, contaminant limits, and mandatory ingredient/additive labeling. GCC introduced new general labelling and microbiological limits requiring verification. India now requires verification of allergen declarations against a new compendium. Taiwan mandates new labeling for 11 specific allergens (e.g., sulphite >= 10 mg/kg). Critical 'source fetch/extraction issues' for labelling in Thailand and the US demand immediate manual review. Moderate-risk additions cover updated guidance on food additives and contaminants (Codex, EU, India, Singapore), new general labelling, Halal, GM product, and shelf-life requirements (Middle East/GCC/GSO), and new labelling/microbiology rules for imports and handling (Japan). Notably, numerous moderate-risk rules across multiple regions (Codex, EU, GCC/GSO, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, Singapore) have been removed, necessitating urgent verification of source changes or data extraction accuracy.

changes.notes: Verification of specific labelling requirements is required by consulting the full regulation.
Verification of specific microbiological limits is required by consulting the full regulation.
Specific mandatory allergen declarations for seasoning, snack food, and ready-to-eat snacks must be verified against this compendium.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited unless specified as unlikely to cause harm by MHLW
changes.notes: Additives (excluding natural flavoring agents and traditional foods used as additives) and preparations/food containing additives are prohibited from sale, production, import, processing, use, storage, or display for sale, unless specified as safe by MHLW.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited if likely to cause harm due to contamination or foreign substances
changes.notes: Food or additives likely to cause harm to human health due to contamination or the addition of foreign substances or on other grounds are prohibited.

changes.parameter: Residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, and pharmaceuticals
changes.notes: Food containing residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, or pharmaceuticals exceeding the quantity specified by MHLW as unlikely to cause harm is prohibited. This forms the basis of the Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited if containing or bearing toxic or harmful substances
changes.notes: Food or additives containing or suspected to contain toxic or harmful substances are prohibited, unless the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies they are unlikely to cause harm.

changes.parameter: Non-conformance to MHLW established standards and criteria
changes.newLimit: Prohibited if non-conforming to MHLW standards or criteria
changes.notes: Food or additives that do not conform to MHLW established standards or criteria are prohibited from production, import, processing, use, cooking, preservation, or sale.

changes.parameter: Diseases or abnormalities in source animals
changes.newLimit: Prohibited for sale/use as food if from diseased/abnormal livestock/poultry
changes.notes: Meat, bones, milk, organs, and blood of livestock or poultry with specified diseases/abnormalities or that have died are prohibited, unless specified by MHLW Order or found safe. Imported products require certificates of sanitation.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited if contaminated with pathogens likely to cause harm
changes.notes: Food or additives contaminated with pathogens or suspected to be so, and which are likely to cause harm to human health, are prohibited.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited if rotten, deteriorated, or immature (unless proven safe)
changes.notes: Food or additives that are rotten, deteriorated, or immature are prohibited from sale, collection, production, import, processing, use, cooking, storage, or display for sale, unless unlikely to cause harm and recognized as edible.
All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.
Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.
Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.
If a food product contains an additive that is not permitted, it must not be imported or sold. An application can be submitted to SFA for evaluation.
All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.
Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
If food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits, they must not be imported or sold.
Import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat containing residues of diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol is prohibited.
All ingredients and additives used in prepacked food products must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, sulphite (as residue sulphur dioxide)
changes.newLimit: Labeling required if present as ingredient or food additive. Sulphite limit: 10 or more mg/kg.
changes.notes: This applies to prepackaged foods containing these allergens as ingredients or food additives.

changes.parameter: Food business operator responsibility for safety and traceability
changes.newLimit: Endeavor to ensure safety, obtain knowledge/skills, ensure raw material safety, conduct voluntary inspections, record necessary information (e.g., seller name), and take prompt measures for hazards.
changes.notes: Food business operators have a responsibility to endeavor to ensure the safety of their products, maintain records for traceability, and take appropriate actions in case of hazards.
This standard provides guidelines for optional shelf life periods for food products. Specific details must be verified by consulting the full standard.
Food additives are allowed when there is a technological justification and they do not present health risks to consumers.
Food businesses must be aware of contaminants commonly found in their products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.
Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

changes.newLimit: Labeling recommended if present as ingredient or food additive.
changes.notes: This applies to prepackaged foods containing these allergens as ingredients or food additives.
